name: fixture-01-project-manager-infrastructure
description: Senior PM (PMP) from transit agency applying for a utility-scale solar farm program. Clean fit on capital-project delivery; stretch is private-sector commercial acumen vs. public-sector background.

resume_data.name: Marcus Chen
key_achievements: Delivered $340M light-rail expansion 4 months ahead of schedule, $12M under budget, across 3 municipalities and 47 private easements.
Closed a 14-month litigation stall by rebuilding the community engagement plan; secured final board approval in 6 weeks.
Built the transit agency's first integrated PMIS (Procore + Oracle Primavera), adopted across 8 active programs.

requirements: 10+ years delivering capital projects in the $100M+ range
Direct experience navigating environmental permitting and community opposition
Strong vendor + contractor management across EPC scope
Ability to partner with finance + development teams on project pro formas
Willing to travel 30-40% to project sites across the Southwest

letter_plan.opening_hook: Lead with the $340M transit project — same dollar scale, same permitting-heavy environment, same multi-jurisdiction coordination the solar role demands — to establish capital-project credibility before the sector stretch.
body_points: $340M delivery + schedule/budget beat demonstrates the capital-project rigor Sunfield's portfolio needs (earned-value, GC oversight, NEPA work).

14-month litigation reversal is direct proof of the community-opposition navigation the JD flags — reframe public-sector stakeholder skill as exactly the capability needed for utility-scale community relations.
Procore + Primavera integration proves the tools-and-systems fluency EPC work demands; acknowledge the sector transition and frame the prior-sector expertise as transferable craft.
letter_plan.closing_strategy: Position the sector transition as a strength — a regulated, permit-intensive, capital-heavy background ports directly to utility-scale solar, and the private-sector pro-forma exposure will be a learning curve of weeks, not years.
